text: Hi, this is Jason Denbo, one of the HPB surgical oncologists at Moffitt Cancer Center. Today's case was an incidental pancreatic mass identified in the tail. This lesion had a thickened wall that had arterial enhancement, and after further workup, the diagnosis was solidified as a non-functional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, approximately two point two centimeters in the tail of the pancreas. We offered a robotic approach. This is the setup. The robot comes in from the right side and you can see four eight millimeter robotic trocars across the mid abdomen and a twelve millimeter assist port. The specimen can be extracted through extension of the supraumbilical port or a fan and steel.

The surgeon uses a DEMA stitch to retract the left lobe of the liver, positioning it like a hammock to maintain exposure. The liver appears small, but the technique is effective for larger segments as well.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer for gastric mobilization. The splenic flexure becomes visible as the surgeon approaches and divides the short gastric vessels with the vessel sealer. Exposure is modified to access the remaining short gastric vessels attached to the superior pole of the spleen. An intraoperative ultrasound is used to identify the tumor and surrounding vasculature, aiding in the decision of the transection plane. The stomach is retracted, and the infrapancreatic margin mobilization begins.

The surgeon uses hook cautery to dissect through an avascular plane, revealing the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure. The third robotic arm and assistant port provide downward traction on the colon. The dissection continues with hook cautery until vessels within the splenocolic ligament are encountered, at which point the vessel sealer is used. The mobilization targets the inferior pole of the spleen, dividing the splenocolic and splenodiaphragmatic attachments. The procedure progresses to the splenic artery dissection.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the planned division site of the splenic artery, encircling it with a vessel loop for control. A vascular stapler is used to divide the artery, followed by completing the suprapancreatic margin mobilization extending to the superior pole of the spleen. Additional mobilization off the retroperitoneum is performed to encircle the pancreas, with the tumor visibly bulging from the posterior pancreas.

The surgeon encircles the pancreas with umbilical tape to manipulate and orient it for division. The pancreas, appearing normal and thick, is divided using a black load stapler, requiring an additional staple load for complete division. Surgicel is applied to the pancreatic transection margin to control bleeding. The retroperitoneal dissection is completed to free the specimen, which is placed in an endo-catch bag and extracted through the supraumbilical port site extension. A drain is then placed.

He did very well and was discharged home on post-update three with a biochemical leak and his drain was removed in clinic. Thank you for watching this video. Please like, comment, and share. And please subscribe to the channel so that you don't miss any of the upcoming new content.
